title: Tool Calling Revolution: How AI Agents Are Breaking Speed Records

ScriptSection(section_id='main', section_type='main', title='How Programmatic Tool Calling Actually Works', narration_text="Okay, let's break this down. Traditional tool calling works like this: you ask the AI to do something, it decides it needs to use a tool, it makes a call, waits for the result, processes it, then continues. Each one of those steps requires a full inference pass. . Programmatic tool calling flips this on its head. The model writes actual code that invokes tools programmatically within a code execution container. Think loops, conditionals, data transformations—all in a single pass. . The result? Dramatically reduced latency for multi-tool workflows because you're batch processing multiple tool calls instead of waiting for round trips. But here's the really clever part: token consumption decreases because the model can filter or process data BEFORE it hits the context window. . You're not just saving time—you're being smart about what data you let into the context window in the first place.", visual_markers=[VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/advanced-tool-use', description=None, section_id='main', focus_text='Advanced Tool Use'), VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents', description=None, section_id='main', focus_text='Effective Context Engineering'), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Code snippet showing a loop calling multiple tools', section_id='main', focus_text=None)], estimated_duration_seconds=58.400000000000006, start_time=48.800000000000004)

ScriptSection(section_id='deep_dive', section_type='deep_dive', title='The Hidden Costs Most Developers Miss', narration_text="Let me tell you about the gotchas that trip up most teams. Your tool definitions and results are processed under standard data retention policies. That's a crucial distinction for enterprise users. . Second, there's a fundamental trade-off to understand. Programmatic calling is efficient for batch operations, but natural language calling is simpler for straightforward tasks. The skill is knowing when to use which. . And finally, the Model Context Protocol—MCP—is an open standard that's rapidly becoming the go-to for connecting AI assistants to data sources. . We're talking content repositories, business tools, development environments—all connected through a standardized protocol. This is the future of AI connectivity.", visual_markers=[VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/programmatic-tool-calling', description=None, section_id='deep_dive', focus_text='code execution must be enabled'), VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol', description=None, section_id='deep_dive', focus_text='Model Context Protocol'), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Warning icon appearing', section_id='deep_dive', focus_text=None), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Decision tree showing when to use each approach', section_id='deep_dive', focus_text=None)], estimated_duration_seconds=52.0, start_time=107.20000000000002)

First: dynamic filtering. . During web searches, Claude can write and execute code to filter results BEFORE loading them into context. This is huge. You get improved accuracy because irrelevant results never make it to the model, and you save tokens because you're processing at the source rather than in the context window. . Second: Agent Skills. . These are reusable, filesystem-based resources that provide domain-specific expertise and load ON-DEMAND. No more repetitive guidance across conversations. . full_text:
Picture this: you're running an AI agent that needs to search the web, analyze data, and execute code—all in one workflow. Traditional tool calling means your AI makes separate round trips for EVERY single tool invocation. That's slow. That's expensive. That's about to change. . Programmatic tool calling is the game-changer that lets AI models write code to invoke tools directly within a code execution container—no more back-and-forth with the model for each tool call. . This isn't just a nice-to-have; it's becoming essential as AI agents handle increasingly complex multi-step workflows. And here's the kicker: tool results and definitions can consume over 50,000 tokens before an agent even reads your actual request. That's why what I'm about to show you matters.

Okay, let's break this down. Traditional tool calling works like this: you ask the AI to do something, it decides it needs to use a tool, it makes a call, waits for the result, processes it, then continues. Each one of those steps requires a full inference pass. . Programmatic tool calling flips this on its head. The model writes actual code that invokes tools programmatically within a code execution container. Think loops, conditionals, data transformations—all in a single pass. . The result? Dramatically reduced latency for multi-tool workflows because you're batch processing multiple tool calls instead of waiting for round trips. But here's the really clever part: token consumption decreases because the model can filter or process data BEFORE it hits the context window. . You're not just saving time—you're being smart about what data you let into the context window in the first place.

Let me tell you about the gotchas that trip up most teams. First, programmatic tool calling requires code execution to be enabled—and here's the important bit—this feature is NOT covered by Zero Data Retention arrangements. . Your tool definitions and results are processed under standard data retention policies. That's a crucial distinction for enterprise users. . Second, there's a fundamental trade-off to understand. Programmatic calling is efficient for batch operations, but natural language calling is simpler for straightforward tasks. The skill is knowing when to use which. . And finally, the Model Context Protocol—MCP—is an open standard that's rapidly becoming the go-to for connecting AI assistants to data sources. . We're talking content repositories, business tools, development environments—all connected through a standardized protocol. This is the future of AI connectivity.

Now let me show you two features that pair beautifully with programmatic tool calling. First: dynamic filtering. . During web searches, Claude can write and execute code to filter results BEFORE loading them into context. This is huge. You get improved accuracy because irrelevant results never make it to the model, and you save tokens because you're processing at the source rather than in the context window. . Second: Agent Skills. . These are reusable, filesystem-based resources that provide domain-specific expertise and load ON-DEMAND. No more repetitive guidance across conversations. . Think of them as specialized knowledge packages your agent can pull in when needed. Combined with programmatic tool calling, you're building agents that are both efficient at execution AND lean on context.

Here's the bottom line: programmatic tool calling isn't just a technical optimization—it's a fundamental shift in how we think about AI agent architecture. You're no longer bound by round-trip latency or wasteful token usage. . The agents that master this capability will be faster, cheaper, and more capable than those relying on traditional approaches. Enable code execution in your setup, experiment with MCP for standardized tool connectivity, and start building dynamic filtering into your search workflows. The future of AI agents is programmatic, efficient, and context-aware. Time to build. .
